---
title: "Where does the data come from?"
subtitle: "Reproducible data management for big neuroimaging"
format: revealjs
---

## {.center}

<br>

### Where does the data come from?

#### Reproducible data management for big neuroimaging

<br>

**AS4SAN Workshop**

::: notes
Welcome. Before we get into analysis pipelines, models, and results — I want to
talk about the step that comes before all of that. Where does the data actually
come from, and how do you keep track of it?
:::

---

## Every other module assumes the data is already here

<br>

> "Download the dataset and place it in `./data/`"
>
> — Every tutorial ever

<br>

- Preprocessing pipelines assume the files exist
- Analysis scripts assume a specific directory layout
- Papers reference "the dataset" but not *which version*

. . .

**Nobody talks about how the data got there — or whether it's the same data you had last week.**

::: notes
This is the gap. Every other part of the workshop — the preprocessing, the
statistical analysis, the machine learning — all of it starts with "assuming
you have the data." But getting the data, knowing which version you have,
and being able to reproduce that exact starting point — that's the hard part
nobody talks about.
:::

---

## The concrete problem: MR-RATE

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Patients** | 83,425 |
| **MRI volumes** | 705,254 |
| **Total size** | 8.1 TB |
| **Modalities** | T1, T2, FLAIR, SWI, MRA |
| **Extras** | radiology reports, brain masks, segmentations |

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**You can't just download 8 TB.**

- Too large for a single machine
- Evolving — new releases, corrections
- Multi-site (Zurich, Istanbul, NVIDIA)
- You only need a *subset* for your analysis

:::
::::

<br>

<small>Hugging Face: `Forithmus/MR-RATE` — CC BY-NC-SA 4.0</small>

::: notes
MR-RATE is a good example of the kind of dataset that breaks the "just download
it" approach. 8 terabytes across 83,000 patients, multiple MRI sequences,
radiology reports, segmentation masks. You can't wget this. You can't put it on
a USB stick. And it changes — new data gets added, errors get corrected. So how
do you work with something like this reproducibly?
:::

---

## The idea: DataLad as version control for data

<br>

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DataLad                        │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐   │
│  │     git      │    │    git-annex     │   │
│  │  tracks:     │    │  tracks:         │   │
│  │  • metadata  │    │  • large files   │   │
│  │  • scripts   │    │  • where to get  │   │
│  │  • history   │    │    them from     │   │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<br>

- **git** for small files (code, metadata, provenance records)
- **git-annex** for large files (images, volumes) — stores *pointers*, not content
- **DataLad** ties them together with a clean command-line interface

::: notes
DataLad builds on two tools you may already know. Git — which you use for code —
tracks small files, history, and metadata. Git-annex extends git to handle large
files by storing pointers instead of the actual content. DataLad wraps both into
a single interface so you don't need to think about which layer is doing what.
The key insight: you can clone a dataset and get all the metadata instantly,
without downloading a single large file.
:::

---

## The core loop: install → get → run → rerun

<br>

| Step | Command | What happens |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **install** | `datalad install -s <url>` | Clone metadata — no large files yet |
| **get** | `datalad get sub-01/anat/*.nii.gz` | Fetch only the files you need |
| **run** | `datalad run -m "msg" -- <cmd>` | Execute & record provenance |
| **rerun** | `datalad rerun` | Reproduce from the recorded provenance |

<br>

<!--
  Asciinema cast embed placeholder.
  Replace demo/casts/demo.cast with your recorded cast, then use one of:

  Option A — asciinema-player (recommended):
    <div id="demo-player"></div>
    <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/asciinema-player@3/dist/bundle/asciinema-player.min.js"></script>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/asciinema-player@3/dist/bundle/asciinema-player.min.css">
    <script>AsciinemaPlayer.create('demo/casts/demo.cast', document.getElementById('demo-player'));</script>

  Option B — fallback linked GIF:
    [![Demo](assets/demo.gif)](demo/casts/demo.cast)
-->
<p style="text-align:center; color:#888; border:2px dashed #ccc; padding:2em;">
  [ asciinema cast will be embedded here — see comments above ]
</p>

::: notes
This is the heart of it. Four commands. Install gives you the full directory
tree and all the metadata, but none of the large files — it's instant even for
terabyte-scale datasets. Get fetches exactly the files you need. Run wraps any
command you'd normally type and records exactly what was run, on which inputs,
producing which outputs. Rerun replays that recorded command — same inputs, same
command, verifiable output. That's the whole workflow.
:::

---

## Why it matters

<br>

### Provenance

> "Which version of which data produced this figure?"

DataLad records the **exact command**, **inputs**, and **outputs** in the git history.

<br>

### Selective access

Download 50 MB for your analysis, not 8 TB for the whole dataset.

<br>

### Multi-site collaboration

Everyone installs the same dataset. `git log` shows who changed what and when.

::: notes
Three reasons this matters in practice. First, provenance — you can go back to
any result and see exactly what data and what command produced it. Second,
efficiency — you only download what you actually need, which is critical when
datasets are terabytes. Third, collaboration — when multiple sites work on the
same dataset, DataLad gives you a shared version history. No more emailing files
or wondering if you're working on the same version.
:::

---

## On-ramp: getting started

<br>

```bash
# Install DataLad (if you haven't already)
pip install datalad
sudo apt install git-annex    # or: brew install git-annex

# Clone the workshop dataset (metadata only — fast)
datalad install -s <workshop-dataset-url> workshop-data

# Fetch the files you need for the exercises
cd workshop-data
datalad get exercises/
```

<br>

You now have exactly the files the notebooks expect — and a record of where they came from.

::: notes
Here's how you get set up for the rest of the workshop. Install DataLad and
git-annex if you don't have them. Then datalad install the workshop dataset —
this is fast because it only pulls metadata. Then datalad get the exercise files.
Now your notebook will just work, and you have a full provenance trail of where
every file came from.
:::

---

## Pointers and resources

<br>

| Resource | Link |
|----------|------|
| **DataLad Handbook** | [handbook.datalad.org](http://handbook.datalad.org) |
| **OpenNeuro** | [openneuro.org](https://openneuro.org) |
| **DataLad datasets** | [datasets.datalad.org](http://datasets.datalad.org) |
| **MR-RATE** | [huggingface.co/datasets/Forithmus/MR-RATE](https://huggingface.co/datasets/Forithmus/MR-RATE) |
| **MR-RATE code** | [github.com/forithmus/MR-RATE](https://github.com/forithmus/MR-RATE) |
| **This talk** | [github.com/arush-arun/AS4SAN-Workshop](https://github.com/arush-arun/AS4SAN-Workshop) |

<br>

The DataLad Handbook is the single best starting point — it's a full tutorial with worked examples.

::: notes
Here are the key resources. The DataLad Handbook is excellent — it walks you
through everything from installation to advanced workflows with real examples.
OpenNeuro hosts thousands of neuroimaging datasets accessible via DataLad.
The MR-RATE dataset is on Hugging Face. And this talk itself is a git repo —
the slides are Quarto markdown, so you can see exactly how they were built.
:::
